15.1.label: Question 15.1
15.1.synopsis: Four areas: (1) Framework for FRC Technical Actuarial Standards (authority, scope, application, Reliability Objective); (2) TAS 100 – Generic standard with six high level outcome-focused principles for all technical actuarial work in FRC's UK geographic scope; (3) Three Specific TASs (TAS 200: Insurance, TAS 300: Pensions, TAS 400: Funeral Plan Trusts); (4) Glossary of defined terms used in the TASs
15.1.question: Outline the four areas of the TAS framework under UK regulation.

The four areas of the TAS framework: The 'Framework for FRC Technical Actuarial Standards'
This sets out the authority, scope and application of the TASs and also sets out the FRC's Reliability Objective.
'Technical Actuarial Standard 100: Principles for Actuarial Work' (TAS 100)

This Generic TAS comprises six high level outcome-focused principles which are applicable to all technical actuarial work carried out within the FRC's UK geographic scope. It sets out the standard of technical actuarial work that users are entitled to expect.
The three Specific TASs (TAS 200: Insurance, TAS 300: Pensions, TAS 400: Funeral Plan Trusts)

The Specific TAS of most relevance to Subject SA1 is TAS 200. Its purpose is to promote high quality technical actuarial work on particular insurance matters where the FRC has identified that there is a high degree of risk to the public interest.
A glossary of defined terms used in the TASs.

15.2.label: Question 15.2
15.2.synopsis: Honesty and integrity, competence and care, clear communications, impartiality, compliance with legal/regulatory/professional requirements, courtesy and professional respect; possibly also speaking up if a course of action is believed to be unethical or unlawful
15.2.question: List six high level principles of professional standards that might be included in an actuarial organisation's code of conduct.

High level principles: • a requirement to act honestly and with the highest standards of integrity
• a requirement to perform professional duties with competence and care
• a requirement to ensure communications are clear, appropriate for the intended audience, and meet all applicable reporting standards
• a requirement to act with impartiality
• a requirement to comply with all relevant legal, regulatory and professional requirements
• a requirement to perform duties with courtesy and professional respect.
There may also be a requirement to speak up if it is believed a course of action is unethical or unlawful.

15.3.label: Question 15.3
15.3.scenario: In some jurisdictions, professional standards are rules-based; in others they are principles-based.

(i).synopsis: Maintain trust in actuary's work, build and promote confidence in actuarial profession, assist with protecting the public interest, provide a benchmark for professional conduct, help actuaries act in difficult situations including conflicts of interest, provide additional guidance on interpretation of regulations, ensure consistency of approach between members, help members justify their actions to others
(i).question: Outline the purposes of professional standards and guidance.

Purposes of professional standards and g: • maintain the trust of those impacted by an actuary's work [½]
• build and promote confidence in the work of actuaries and in the actuarial profession [½]
• provide a benchmark against which professional conduct can be measured [½]
• help actuaries work out how to act in difficult situations, ... [½]
... such as where there are conflicts of interest [½]
• provide additional guidance on the interpretation of regulations where there is otherwise insufficient clarity [½]
• ensure consistency of approach between members [½]
• help members to justify their actions to others (eg client, employer) in difficult situations. [½]

(ii).synopsis: Rules-based advantages: clearer how to act, less open to individual interpretation so more consistent, easier to judge misconduct. Principles-based advantages: impossible to have rules covering every situation given wide variety of actuarial roles, gives more weight to actuarial judgement and individual professionalism, principles easier to keep up to date especially as actuarial roles evolve into wider fields
(ii).question: Discuss the relative merits of these two approaches.

Relative advantages of a rules-based app: • it can be clearer how to act in a certain situation (principles can be vague) [½]
• prescriptive rules are less open to individual interpretation, thus enabling more consistency in application [1]
• it may be easier to judge misconduct against rules rather than principles. [½]
Relative advantages of a principles-base: • it is impossible to have a set of rules that covers every possible situation, ... [½]
... given the wide variety of roles that actuaries carry out [½]
• it gives more weight to the use of actuarial judgement, thus respecting the professionalism of individuals [½]
• principles are easier to keep up to date than rules, ... [½]
... particularly where actuarial roles are evolving into wider fields. [½]
